Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine in the traditional sense. At 28, I've got a lot going on outside of uni, so I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could - maybe while waiting for something or during quiet moments at home. I learn best by doing things hands-on anyway, so sitting down with a schedule never worked for me. I'd say my approach was more spontaneous than organized.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd have a burst of energy and engage more. Deadlines didn't even push me that much since I only completed about 11% of assignments anyway. Life stuff - work, social commitments, my extracurriculars - those dictated when I studied more than any academic calendar did.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got a notification or remembered something was happening in the course. Sometimes I'd pop in just to see what my peers were discussing because that social element kept things interesting. Other times it was pure curiosity or boredom scrolling. It wasn't really about obligation for me.
Q4: Yeah, there was a period where checking the LMS became almost like checking social media for me. I'd log in pretty frequently - maybe 19 times over the course - just to see what was new or what people were saying. The peer interactions made it feel less like homework and more like a community space. That's what kept me coming back.
Q5: The videos were genuinely useful - I watched quite a lot of them, probably over three hours worth. As a kinesthetic learner, I liked being able to pause and try things out while watching. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing different perspectives from other students helped concepts click in ways that just reading never did for me.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and written assignments. They felt dry and passive, which doesn't suit how I learn. If something required me to just sit and absorb text without any interaction or hands-on element, I'd put it off indefinitely. The task completion stuff suffered because of this - I just couldn't make myself care about busywork.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to something practical or if it involved other people. Peer discussions, collaborative stuff, videos showing real applications - those grabbed me. If something felt like it was just ticking a box or wouldn't actually help me understand anything better, I'd skip it without guilt.
Q8: Required activities honestly felt like a burden, which is probably why my completion rate was so low. Optional stuff felt more like an invitation, and paradoxically I was sometimes more drawn to those. When something's optional, I can engage with it on my terms, which suits my independent streak. Required stuff made me feel boxed in.
Q9: Absolutely, loads of my learning happened offline. I'd download videos or materials and work through them later without being connected. I also used external resources constantly - YouTube tutorials, industry blogs, hands-on practice with real tools. The LMS was just one piece of a much bigger learning puzzle for me.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through concepts by actually doing things - building, practicing, experimenting - that the LMS would never capture. I might watch a video once online, then spend hours offline trying to apply what I learned. My exam score of 74% came from understanding gained through doing, not from logged activity.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd access something once, download it or take notes, and then that material would live in my own system. I'd revisit my notes or the downloaded files multiple times without ever logging back in. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the actual learning that happened afterward.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't be bothered. Usually it was when life got busy with other things - my extracurriculars, social stuff, just general life as a 28-year-old. But honestly, I never felt super stressed about it because my resilience is pretty high. I'd disengage, then bounce back when I felt ready.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the big one for me. I've got a full life outside of this course, and sometimes the LMS just fell down the priority list. Fatigue played a role too - after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to log in and complete tasks. Confusion wasn't really an issue; I just had other things going on.
Q14: Oh definitely. Sometimes I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd watch a video but realize I hadn't absorbed anything. Those sessions felt hollow - I was present in the system but not really engaged with the content. It was more like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. Part of it was that they didn't feel urgent enough compared to everything else in my life. Part of it was that written assignments don't suit my learning style, so I'd avoid them in favor of things I actually enjoyed. I'm pretty good at rationalizing procrastination.
Q16: Deadlines barely moved the needle for me, honestly. I'd sometimes notice them approaching and still not act. When I did engage close to deadlines, it was usually a last-minute scramble rather than planned effort. My low assignment completion shows that deadlines alone weren't enough to motivate me - I needed the content itself to grab me.
Q17: Feedback from peers in discussions actually motivated me more than formal feedback on assignments. When someone responded to my post or built on my ideas, that felt rewarding and made me want to engage more. Official feedback on the few assignments I did submit didn't really change my behavior much.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty flexible, which suited me in some ways but also let me drift. If it had been more rigid, I might have engaged differently - or I might have just dropped out entirely. The peer support intervention helped because it added a social element that made participation feel more meaningful to me.
Q19: The video content and discussion forums made engagement easier because they suited my learning style. What made it harder was the emphasis on written assignments and the sheer volume of reading materials. If there had been more hands-on projects or practical applications, I would have been way more invested.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was on the LMS a lot, engaging with videos and discussions, but my assignment submissions were minimal. For me, it was about learning what interested me rather than completing everything for grades. Some people might also be exploring, procrastinating, or just not connecting with the assessment format.
Q21: That's basically my story too. My attendance was terrible and my assignment completion was low, but I still got a Merit. I learned a lot through the videos I watched, the peer discussions, and especially through offline practice and external resources. The LMS activity doesn't capture the full picture of what I actually learned.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually thinking deeply or just scrolling mindlessly. They definitely miss all the offline learning, the conversations with peers outside the platform, the real-world application of concepts.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and wanting to understand it deeply. It's not about ticking boxes or completing every task. It's about curiosity, making connections, discussing ideas with others, and applying knowledge to real situations. You can be highly engaged without a perfect completion record.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through hands-on practice outside the platform, or how the peer support really lifted my spirits and kept me connected to the course. It wouldn't capture the three-hour conversations I had with classmates about course concepts, or how happy and unstressed I generally felt throughout. My engagement was real - it just looked different from what the data would suggest.
